name: Mandrake
description: The Mandrake, a plant of the nightshade family, holds a potent place in European folklore and magical traditions. Its human-like root, often resembling a male or female form, is believed to shriek when uprooted, a sound said to drive listeners mad or even kill them. This scream necessitates specialized harvesting techniques, often involving dogs to pull the root while the gatherer remains at a safe distance, or plugging one's ears with wax. The Mandrake is associated with both witchcraft and healing. It was used in potions for fertility, love, and protection, as well as for anesthesia and pain relief. Its association with witches stems from its potent psychoactive properties and its use in flying ointments. The plant's dual nature, capable of both harm and healing, made it a highly valued and feared ingredient in magical practices. Symbolically, the Mandrake represents life, death, and rebirth, reflecting its connection to both the underworld and the fertile earth. Its cultural importance lies in its perceived power to bridge the gap between the physical and spiritual realms, making it a key component in rituals and spells throughout European history. The plant's rarity and the dangers associated with its harvest added to its mystique and value, solidifying its place in the collective imagination as a powerful magical herb. Beliefs surrounding the Mandrake's origin often link it to the semen of hanged men, further reinforcing its connection to death and the supernatural. It continues to fascinate and inspire, appearing in modern fantasy literature and film as a symbol of powerful magic and hidden knowledge.
subtitle: Screaming root of magic and madness

keyMyths.title: The Screaming Mandrake
keyMyths.description: The Mandrake screams when uprooted, a sound fatal to those who hear it. To avoid this, people would use dogs to pull the root out, sacrificing the animal instead of themselves.

keyMyths.title: Mandrake's Origin
keyMyths.description: Some believe Mandrakes grow where the semen of hanged men falls. This links the plant to death, the underworld, and potent magic.

sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: Describes the Mandrake's medicinal properties and harvesting methods.
sources.text: Describes the Mandrake's medicinal properties and harvesting methods.

sources.author: Nicholas Culpeper
sources.description: Details the Mandrake's astrological associations and uses in herbal medicine.
